era_context: The Han Dynasty is in its final decades, weakened by court factionalism and eunuch dominance. The Roman Empire under Commodus faces internal conspiracies and military pressure. In India, the Satavahana Dynasty controls the Deccan. This is a period of imperial overextension and the emergence of forces that will reshape Eurasia—religious movements, military strongmen, and barbarian pressure on imperial frontiers.

events.title: Han Dynasty administrative deterioration continues
events.description: The Han central government's authority continues to weaken as provincial governors gain autonomy and local strongmen accumulate power. Tax collection becomes irregular, and the imperial treasury is depleted by military spending and court expenses. This administrative collapse creates the conditions for the Yellow Turban Rebellion that will erupt in 184–185 CE. The deterioration is gradual but accelerating.

sources.name: Book of the Later Han (Hou Han Shu)

events.certainty_note: Administrative deterioration is well-attested for the late Han period, but specific events in 182 CE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Han decline from 160s CE onward

events.title: Commodus faces conspiracy and consolidates power
events.description: Emperor Commodus continues to face conspiracies from senators and military rivals. He responds by consolidating autocratic power, relying increasingly on the Praetorian Guard and military strongmen. This year marks a continuation of the political instability that characterizes his reign, with purges of suspected enemies and the elevation of military favorites.
events.certainty_note: Commodus's political instability is well-attested, but specific events in 182 CE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Han decline as purely political is complicated by recent scholarship emphasizing economic vitality and cultural flourishing even as political authority fragmented. The characterization of Commodus as purely incompetent is also debated—some scholars argue he was a capable administrator dealing with impossible circumstances.
historiographic_note: Han sources are written by the literati faction, which had reason to blame eunuchs and the emperor for decline. Roman sources on Commodus are heavily biased by senators he antagonized. The exact chronology of events in 182 CE is uncertain.

graph_edges.note: Administrative deterioration enabled the Yellow Turban Rebellion
